content: {"perspective": "Economic", "answer": "The search results provide partial specifics on the FMD-in-T2D cost-utility study. Source 2 (ScienceDirect, doi S1098301524028468) and Source 9 (PubMed 39343090) — the same paper with two links — disclose the key effectiveness claims: the FMD programme was associated with '11.4% less diabetes complications, 67.2% less overall diabetes medication use' versus standard care in the cost-utility analysis. These two effect magnitudes are the closest the snippets get to ICER inputs. Source 14 (Semantic Scholar summary of the Burg-Peet paper, 18766acd9ac87249e9e2c72d1e4ce9fc9f2acfd4) is CRITICAL: it explicitly concludes 'The FMD programme does not appear to be cost-effective over the first year of its application in the context of the Dutch healthcare system, and adding an [extrapolation]...' — which directly answers the economic question with an unfavorable short-term verdict. Source 6 (Consultant360 conference coverage) reports that a real-world study of the FMD programme showed 'improved glycemic control, medication reduction, and weight loss in people' — descriptively confirming the same three outcomes. Source 15 (FIT trial thesis PDF) discloses the methodological conventions used: 'In this lifetime cost-utility analysis costs will be discounted at a percentage of 4% and effects at a percentage of 1.5%, according to the Dutch guidelines' — this is consistent with Dutch HTA convention but differs from NICE (3.5% for both costs and effects) and ICER ($50,000-$150,000 per QALY with 3% discount on both). Source 5 (Leiden University thesis PDF) confirms the study is part of a programmatic T2D-FMD research line. Sources 1, 3, 4, 7, 11, 12, 13 are all metadata-only references to the same Burg et al. paper. Source 10 (ResearchGate 384414696) describes a US cost-utility analysis of FMD vs standard care in diabetic patients on dual or triple medications — a second economic evaluation but no ICER disclosed. Information gap: specific ICER per QALY, net monetary benefit, incremental costs, and probabilistic sensitivity analysis results are NOT in any snippet. The only directly quoted economic verdict is Source 14's statement that FMD is NOT cost-effective over the first year in the Dutch context — this is the most actionable finding. The lifetime extrapolation is mentioned but its results are not disclosed in the snippets, meaning the question cannot be fully answered without full-text reading.", "key_findings": ["Burg-Peet FMD-in-T2D cost-utility analysis (Sources 2, 9): FMD was associated with 11.4% fewer diabetes complications and 67.2% less overall diabetes medication use versus standard care — the key effectiveness inputs.", "Semantic Scholar summary (Source 14) explicitly states: 'The FMD programme does not appear to be cost-effective over the first year of its application in the context of the Dutch healthcare system' — the only direct economic verdict recovered.", "Dutch cost-utility convention used (Source 15, FIT trial thesis): 4% discount on costs, 1.5% discount on effects — differs from NICE (3.5% both) and US ICER (3% both), so cross-jurisdiction benchmarking is non-trivial.", "Source 10 (ResearchGate 384414696) describes a separate US-setting cost-utility analysis of FMD vs standard care in T2D patients on dual/triple medications — a second economic evaluation but with no ICER disclosed in the snippet.", "Information gap: specific ICER per QALY, net monetary benefit, incremental cost components, and probabilistic sensitivity analysis results are NOT in any recovered snippet — full-text reading of Burg et al. is required to answer the question quantitatively."], "sources_used":
